questions.question: What is the key difference between healthy conflict and coercive control?

options.text: Healthy conflict is calm; coercive control involves yelling
In healthy conflict you can disagree without fear; in coercive control disagreement is punished and power is profoundly imbalanced
Healthy conflict happens rarely; coercive control means fighting every day
There is no meaningful difference — all relationship conflict is the same

questions.explanation: Healthy conflict (even intense): both people can express their perspective, goal is resolution/understanding, repair happens after rupture, you feel heard even if you disagree, power is relatively balanced, you can disagree without fear. Coercive control: one person's perspective dominates consistently, goal is compliance (not resolution), no genuine repair (pattern continues), you feel silenced/dismissed/crazy, power is profoundly imbalanced, disagreement is punished. The test: In healthy conflict, you can say 'I disagree' and be heard. In coercive control, saying 'I disagree' leads to punishment, intimidation, or gaslighting.

questions.question: What is DARVO and how does it function in abusive relationships?

options.text: A conflict resolution technique taught in couples therapy
Deny, Attack, Reverse Victim and Offender — a manipulation tactic where the abuser becomes the 'victim' and you become the 'aggressor'
A communication style that's the same as normal defensiveness
A personality disorder diagnosis for controlling individuals

questions.explanation: DARVO (Deny, Attack, Reverse Victim and Offender) is a documented manipulation tactic: (1) Deny: 'I never said that. That didn't happen.' (2) Attack: 'You're too sensitive. You're crazy.' (3) Reverse: 'I'm the one who's hurt here. YOU'RE abusing ME.' Why it's effective: you start defending yourself instead of addressing the original issue, you question your own reality (gaslighting effect), the abuser becomes the 'victim,' and outsiders may believe the abuser's version. Unlike normal defensiveness (which calms down eventually), DARVO is a strategic pattern used to evade accountability and control the narrative.

questions.question: Your partner consistently monitors your phone, criticizes your friends until you stopped seeing them, and tells you you're 'too sensitive' when you express hurt. You've learned assertiveness skills in this course. What is the appropriate response?

options.text: Use your new assertiveness skills to set clearer boundaries with your partner
Suggest couples therapy to improve your communication together
Recognize this is coercive control (not poor communication) — assertiveness can escalate danger; safety planning with specialists is appropriate
Try harder to communicate your needs more clearly until they understand

questions.explanation: The pattern described (monitoring, isolation, gaslighting) is coercive control — intentional domination, not poor communication. In coercively controlling relationships, assertiveness often escalates danger because the abuser interprets boundary-setting as defiance or loss of control and responds by tightening control or punishing you. Couples therapy is contraindicated (dangerous) in abusive relationships — abusers weaponize therapy language. The appropriate response: safety planning, advocacy, legal support, and specialist intervention (National Domestic Violence Hotline: 1-800-799-7233). Assertiveness skills are designed for relationships with mutual respect — abuse is not a relationship problem; it's a power and control problem requiring different interventions.

questions.question: When is assertiveness training contraindicated (insufficient and potentially dangerous) as a primary intervention?

options.text: When you're naturally introverted and prefer alone time
When you're in an abusive or coercively controlling relationship where communication can escalate danger
When you have any disagreement with a family member
Assertiveness training is always appropriate for any boundary problem

questions.explanation: In abusive or coercively controlling relationships, assertiveness training is contraindicated as a primary intervention — it can escalate danger. The abuser is not struggling with communication skills; they are intentionally controlling you. Safety planning, advocacy, and specialist support are needed instead. A 2021 systematic review of safety planning with intimate partner violence (IPV) survivors (17 studies) found that effective interventions were empowerment-and-advocacy focused — NOT communication skills training. Additionally, boundary difficulties rooted in severe trauma histories (C-PTSD, complex attachment wounds) require therapeutic processing, not just skill instruction.

questions.question: A 2025 Australian national survey found what alarming statistic about young people's understanding of coercive control?

options.text: Over 25% of young people aged 14-20 confuse controlling behaviors with 'healthy boundaries'
Most young people can accurately identify coercive control in all situations
Young people are not affected by coercive control patterns
100% of young people have received adequate psychoeducation about abuse

questions.explanation: A 2025 Australian national survey found that over 25% of young people aged 14-20 confuse controlling behaviors with 'healthy boundaries' — highlighting the critical need for coercive control education at the psychoeducational level. This is why this lesson exists: to help learners recognize the difference between assertiveness in mutually respectful relationships versus coercive control patterns (monitoring, isolation, gaslighting, DARVO, financial control, reproductive coercion, threats). Coercive control is an ongoing, repetitive pattern of behaviors designed to establish dominance and induce psychological, practical, and social dependency — distinct from conflict or poor communication.

title: Recognizing Coercive and Manipulative Patterns ⚠️ SAFETY CRITICAL - Quiz
